articles.title: A New Pill for Sleep Apnea
articles.summary: Scientists have found a pill that might help people with sleep apnea, a condition where breathing stops during sleep. The pill is called sulthiame. In a study with 298 people in Europe, some took the pill and others took a fake pill. Those who took sulthiame had fewer breathing problems at night and slept better. The pill works by helping the body control breathing and keeping the airway open. It has mild side effects. This is exciting because many people with sleep apnea use a mask called CPAP, but it can be uncomfortable. The pill could be a new way to help them. Researchers are happy with the results and plan to do more studies.

articles.title: Tiny Plant Trick Could Help Grow More Food
articles.summary: Scientists found a cool trick in a small plant called hornwort that might help us grow more food like wheat and rice. Plants use sunlight to make food through photosynthesis, but an enzyme called Rubisco is slow and gets confused by oxygen, which wastes energy. Hornworts have a special protein called RbcS-STAR that makes Rubisco stick together in clusters, helping it work better. This is like a natural carbon concentrating system. Since hornworts are more similar to crops than algae, this trick could be easier to copy into food plants to make them grow faster and produce more food for people.

articles.title: Chemicals from Pet Flea Treatments Harm River Animals
articles.summary: Scientists found that chemicals used in flea treatments for pets like dogs and cats are getting into rivers in Wales and hurting wildlife. These chemicals come from washing pets or their bedding, and they go down drains into rivers. The study looked at 62 places in nine rivers over three years and found the chemicals in most samples, especially in cities. The chemicals are called imidacloprid and fipronil, and they are not allowed for farming but are still used on pets. This pollution is worse in urban areas because of sewer problems. Vets are now advising pet owners to be careful with flea treatments to protect rivers.

articles.title: A Giant Space Rock Hit the North Sea and Made a Huge Wave
articles.summary: Scientists have solved a big mystery about a hole under the North Sea called Silverpit Crater. They found out that a huge asteroid or comet crashed there about 45 million years ago. This crash made a giant tsunami wave as tall as a 30-story building. The crater is hidden deep under the sea, and for years, people argued about how it formed. Some thought it was from an asteroid, while others said it was from salt or volcanoes moving underground. Now, new pictures and rock samples show it was definitely an asteroid impact. The scientists used special tools to look at the rocks and make computer models. This discovery helps us learn more about Earth's history and how space rocks can change our planet.

articles.title: How Cannabis Can Trick Your Memory
articles.summary: A new study shows that cannabis, a drug from the marijuana plant, can make people remember things that never happened. Scientists at Washington State University tested 120 people who use cannabis regularly. They gave some people a fake version and others real cannabis with THC, the chemical that makes you feel high. After taking it, the people did memory tests for about an hour. The tests checked different types of memory, like remembering words or where information came from. People who had THC did worse on most tests. They often said they heard words that were never said and had trouble remembering where they learned things. This means cannabis can create false memories and confuse your memory, which might be a problem in important situations like remembering what you saw as a witness. The study found that even a little THC can affect memory a lot, and it doesn't matter if you take more or less.

articles.title: AI Data Centers Might Slow Down New Homes
articles.summary: The government has a new idea to help AI data centers get electricity faster. AI data centers are big buildings that use a lot of power for computers. Right now, all new projects like hospitals and homes must wait in a line to get electricity. This line has gotten very long because of data centers, so some projects wait for years. The government wants to let projects that create jobs and help the economy skip the line. This includes AI data centers, electric car charging stations, and factories. But home builders are worried. They say if new homes are not in the priority list, it could stop new homes from being built. They think this is unfair because we need more houses for families. Data centers already get special treatment in planning rules. The government says this change will help Britain grow and be fairer with AI.
